Query: A 40-year-old man presents to his primary-care doctor for a follow-up of his hypertension. He is asymptomatic at his office visit and denies any new complaints. He has a 10-year history of hypertension that remains poorly controlled on maximum doses of lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. His past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He has no smoking history, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies any illicit drug use. His father required a kidney transplant in his forties. The physical exam is notable for palpable flank masses bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a creatinine of 2.5. The physician orders a renal ultrasound, and the results are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate test to screen for additional complications of this patient's condition?

Options:
A: Colonoscopy
B: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy
C: Liver function tests
D: MR angiography of the brain

Document [24] (Title: Microalbuminuria -- History and Physical -- History Taking) Microalbuminuria, in itself, has not been associated with any specific symptoms. Additionally, patients with diabetes may present with symptoms of cardiac disease, vision difficulties, and urinary tract disorders. On physical exam, it is particularly important to evaluate for elevated blood pressure, abnormal cardiac exam, carotid pulse for bruits, and lower extremity swelling. [11]

These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.
